activities_projects.title: Static Electricity Discovery
activities_projects.objective: To understand that atoms contain charged particles[cite: 21].
steps: Comb your dry hair and bring the comb near small pieces of paper[cite: 18].
Rub a glass rod with silk cloth and bring it near an inflated balloon[cite: 19].
Observe if the objects attract each other and discuss where the charge comes from[cite: 20, 21].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that atoms are divisible and consist of charged particles[cite: 21].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the comb attracts paper after rubbing it on hair (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Thomson’s Watermelon Model
activities_projects.objective: To visualize J.J. Thomson’s model of an atom[cite: 45].
materials: A slice of watermelon (or drawing materials)
steps: Compare an atom to a watermelon where the red part is the positive charge[cite: 48].
Place 'seeds' (beads) to represent electrons studded in the positive sphere[cite: 48, 49].
Discuss why the atom is neutral as a whole[cite: 71].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that in Thomson's model, electrons are embedded in a positively charged sphere[cite: 66, 70].
activities_projects.evaluation: State the two main postulates of Thomson’s model [cite: 65, 66, 71] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Rutherford’s Scattering Analogy
activities_projects.objective: To understand why most alpha particles passed through the gold foil[cite: 88, 105].
steps: Throw stones at a solid wall and listen for the sound[cite: 100].
Throw stones at a barbed-wire fence and observe how many pass through[cite: 102].
Relate the gaps in the fence to the empty space inside an atom[cite: 103, 105].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that most of the space inside an atom is empty[cite: 105].
activities_projects.evaluation: Why did Rutherford select a gold foil for his experiment? [cite: 75] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Bohr’s Energy Levels Sketch
activities_projects.objective: To draw and label the discrete orbits of an atom[cite: 128, 129].
steps: Draw a small center for the nucleus[cite: 149].
Draw four concentric circles around it[cite: 150].
Label the shells as K, L, M, and N or n=1, 2, 3, and 4[cite: 151].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that electrons revolve in specific shells without radiating energy[cite: 130, 133].
activities_projects.evaluation: Draw a sketch of Bohr’s model with three shells [cite: 137] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Sub-atomic Particle Comparison
activities_projects.objective: To identify and compare electrons, protons, and neutrons[cite: 145].
steps: Create cards for Electron (e), Proton (p), and Neutron (n)[cite: 28, 143].
Mark their charges: -1, +1, and 0[cite: 29, 30, 141].
Compare their masses: Proton and Neutron are 1 unit each, while Electron is negligible[cite: 29, 30, 313].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the three fundamental particles that make up an atom[cite: 307].
activities_projects.evaluation: Which sub-atomic particle was discovered by J. Chadwick? [cite: 141] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: The $2n^2$ Rule Practice
activities_projects.objective: To calculate the maximum number of electrons in a shell[cite: 156, 161].
steps: Use the formula $2n^2$ where 'n' is the shell number[cite: 161].
Calculate for K-shell ($n=1$), L-shell ($n=2$), and M-shell ($n=3$)[cite: 162].
Verify the results: 2, 8, and 18[cite: 162].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of the rules for electron distribution in different orbits[cite: 154, 155].
activities_projects.evaluation: What is the maximum number of electrons that can be in the outermost orbit? [cite: 163] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Electronic Configuration Modeling
activities_projects.objective: To display the atomic structure of first eighteen elements[cite: 198].
steps: Choose an element like Sodium ($Z=11$) or Carbon ($Z=6$)[cite: 207].
Draw the nucleus and shells[cite: 195].
Place 'electrons' (stickers) in shells starting from the inner K-shell[cite: 164, 165].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes how electrons are distributed in shells like 2, 8, 1 for Sodium[cite: 386].
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the distribution of electrons in a Lithium atom [cite: 207] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Valency Calculator
activities_projects.objective: To determine the combining capacity of elements[cite: 172, 315].
steps: Identify the number of valence electrons (outermost shell)[cite: 174].
If valence electrons are $\le 4$, valency = valence electrons (e.g., Magnesium = 2)[cite: 217, 218].
If valence electrons are $> 4$, subtract from 8 (e.g., Fluorine: $8-7=1$)[cite: 220, 224].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands how atoms react to achieve a full octet in the outermost shell[cite: 210, 212].
activities_projects.evaluation: What is the valency of Chlorine? [cite: 207, 230] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Atomic vs. Mass Number
steps: Define Atomic Number (Z) as the number of protons[cite: 234, 237].
Define Mass Number (A) as the sum of protons and neutrons[cite: 245].
Practice the notation ${}_{Z}^{A}X$ using Nitrogen ${}_{7}^{14}N$[cite: 246, 255].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that mass of an atom resides in the nucleus[cite: 241].
activities_projects.evaluation: If an atom has 8 protons and 8 neutrons, find its Mass Number [cite: 245, 258] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Isotope Matching
activities_projects.objective: To learn about atoms of the same element with different masses[cite: 259, 260].
materials: Diagrams of Protium, Deuterium, and Tritium [cite: 261, 292]
steps: Compare the three isotopes of Hydrogen[cite: 265].
Observe that they all have 1 proton but different numbers of neutrons[cite: 262].
Discuss uses of isotopes, like Cobalt for cancer treatment[cite: 281, 286].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that isotopes have the same atomic number but different mass numbers[cite: 264, 319].
activities_projects.evaluation: Name the three isotopes of Hydrogen [cite: 261, 265] (10 marks).
